snippets.title: Regression: continuous target with MSE loss (scikit-learn)
snippets.code:
from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression
from sklearn.metrics import mean_absolute_error

# y is continuous: house prices in dollars
X = [[1200, 3], [800, 2], [1500, 4]]
y = [250_000, 180_000, 310_000]

model = LinearRegression()
model.fit(X, y)
y_hat = model.predict([[1100, 3]])
print("predicted price:", round(y_hat[0]))
print("MAE on train:", mean_absolute_error(y, model.predict(X)))
snippets.explanation: Linear regression outputs a real-valued ŷ; MAE/RMSE compare numeric predictions to numeric labels.

snippets.title: Binary classification: discrete labels with log loss (scikit-learn)
snippets.code:
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score, log_loss

# y is discrete: 0 = ham, 1 = spam
X = [[0.1, 0.9], [0.8, 0.2], [0.7, 0.3], [0.2, 0.8]]
y = [1, 0, 0, 1]

clf = LogisticRegression()
clf.fit(X, y)
proba = clf.predict_proba(X)[:, 1]   # P(spam | x)
preds = clf.predict(X)               # class labels
print("accuracy:", accuracy_score(y, preds))
print("log loss:", log_loss(y, proba))
snippets.explanation: Logistic regression is classification: predict_proba returns class probabilities; predict returns discrete labels.

snippets.title: Same features, wrong problem type: MSE on class labels
snippets.code:
from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression
from sklearn.metrics import mean_squared_error

# Digit labels 0-9 treated as numbers — usually wrong framing
X = [[0.1, 0.9], [0.8, 0.2], [0.3, 0.7]]
y_class = [2, 7, 2]  # digit labels

reg = LinearRegression().fit(X, y_class)
y_hat = reg.predict(X)
print("MSE:", mean_squared_error(y_class, y_hat))
print("rounded preds:", [round(v) for v in y_hat])
# Predicting 3.5 as a "digit" has no valid meaning; use softmax + cross-entropy instead.
snippets.explanation: Applying regression loss to unordered class IDs treats categories as equally spaced numbers — a common mistake when problem type is misidentified.

snippets.title: PyTorch: regression head vs classification head
snippets.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

features = torch.tensor([[1.0, 2.0], [2.0, 3.0]])

# Regression head: one real output, MSE loss
reg_head = nn.Linear(2, 1)
y_reg = torch.tensor([[5.0], [8.0]])
loss_reg = nn.MSELoss()(reg_head(features), y_reg)

# Classification head: logits -> softmax, cross-entropy
clf_head = nn.Linear(2, 3)  # 3 classes
y_cls = torch.tensor([0, 2])  # class indices
loss_cls = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()(clf_head(features), y_cls)

print("regression loss:", loss_reg.item())
print("classification loss:", loss_cls.item())
snippets.explanation: The final layer and loss must match target type: linear + MSE for continuous y, linear logits + cross-entropy for discrete class indices.
